agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA presentation from exact timestamps to date-only windows. This is a customer-facing change that affects how recipients and senders perceive delivery commitments. In logistics, ETA precision directly correlates with customer satisfaction, operational accountability, and contractual SLA enforcement. Reducing precision from timestamp to date-level is a significant domain change that affects the entire delivery promise chain.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking & ETA Communication — the context responsible for computing, storing, and presenting estimated delivery times to customers and internal stakeholders.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — the projected datetime (or date) when a parcel will be delivered to the recipient."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"The time range within which delivery is promised. Currently an implicit point-in-time; proposed change makes it a full calendar day."},{"term":"Delivery Promise","definition":"The commitment made to the customer about when their delivery will arrive. This is what the customer sees and holds the company accountable to."},{"term":"SLA (Service Level Agreement)","definition":"Contractual obligation defining delivery performance targets, often tied to time precision (e.g., next-day by noon, 2-hour window)."},{"term":"Last Mile","definition":"The final leg of delivery from distribution center to recipient, where ETA precision matters most."},{"term":"On-Time Delivery Rate","definition":"KPI measuring percentage of deliveries completed within the promised window. Changing window granularity directly affects this metric."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A delivery promise, once communicated to a customer, represents a commitment the organization is measured against.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If the system can silently change or weaken a delivery promise after communication, the trust contract with the customer is broken and metrics become meaningless.","testable_assertion":"Once an ETA is displayed to a customer, any change to that ETA must be communicated as an update, not silently replaced."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"ETA precision must be consistent with the service tier purchased. Premium/express services require tighter delivery windows than standard services.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If a customer pays for express delivery with a 2-hour window but sees only a date, the service tier distinction is destroyed — a fundamental integrity violation.","testable_assertion":"Given a premium service tier with a sub-day SLA, the ETA display must reflect sub-day precision."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Reducing ETA precision must not violate existing contractual SLA commitments with B2B customers.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Contractual obligations are legal constraints. Unilaterally degrading ETA precision could constitute breach of contract.","testable_assertion":"Given a B2B customer with a contract specifying time-of-day delivery reporting, the system must continue providing time-of-day precision to that customer."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"On-Time Delivery Rate KPI calculation must use the same granularity as the delivery promise shown to the customer.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If the promise says 'Friday' but the KPI measures against 14:30, the metric is decoupled from the customer experience and becomes unreliable for decision-making.","testable_assertion":"When ETA is displayed as date-only, on-time delivery is measured as 'delivered anytime on that date'; when displayed as timestamp, on-time delivery is measured against that timestamp."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"The display format of ETAs is a business policy that may vary by service tier, customer segment, or channel.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"A VP could decide to show timestamps for premium customers and date-only for standard — this is a policy lever, not a system integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"The system can be configured to show different ETA formats per service tier without code changes."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer viewing a delivery with a standard service tier","when":"The ETA is displayed","then":"The ETA is shown as a date-only window (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday') if and only if the service tier does not contractually require time-of-day precision."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A customer viewing a delivery with a premium or express service tier","when":"The ETA is displayed","then":"The ETA must retain sub-day precision (e.g., time window or exact timestamp) consistent with the service tier's SLA."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A B2B customer with a contract specifying time-of-day delivery reporting","when":"Delivery tracking data is displayed or exported","then":"Time-of-day precision is preserved regardless of the default display format change."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The ETA display format changes from timestamp to date-only for a delivery","when":"The On-Time Delivery Rate KPI is calculated","then":"The KPI uses date-level granularity for that delivery's on-time assessment."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A delivery is in transit and the customer previously saw a timestamp-based ETA","when":"The system transitions to date-only display","then":"The customer is not shown a less-precise ETA for an already-in-flight delivery without explicit notification of the change."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Cross-midnight deliveries: A package ETA is 23:45 Friday. Under date-only, 'Delivery by Friday' is technically correct but leaves almost no margin. If delivered at 00:15 Saturday, is it late?","expected_behavior":"The system must define whether 'by Friday' means by 23:59:59 Friday or by the end of the delivery operations window on Friday. This must be explicit."},{"scenario":"Time-zone boundaries: Customer in one timezone, warehouse in another. 'Delivery by Friday' — whose Friday?","expected_behavior":"Date-only display must be anchored to the recipient's local timezone, and this must be clearly defined."},{"scenario":"Same-day delivery: Customer orders with same-day delivery. Showing 'Delivery by Today' without a time is functionally useless.","expected_behavior":"Same-day and express tiers must retain time-of-day precision. Date-only format is incompatible with same-day service."},{"scenario":"Multiple delivery attempts: First attempt fails Friday morning. Is the 'by Friday' promise broken, or does the courier have until end-of-day Friday?","expected_behavior":"The promise window extends to end-of-day. A failed first attempt within the promised date does not count as a missed SLA if re-attempted and delivered same day."},{"scenario":"B2B API consumers parsing ETA fields: Downstream systems expecting ISO 8601 datetime may break if they receive date-only values.","expected_behavior":"API contracts must not change format without versioning. Existing integrations must continue receiving datetime precision until migrated."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Reducing ETA precision could artificially inflate On-Time Delivery Rate (wider window = easier to hit), masking operational inefficiencies and delaying investment in real improvements. Conversely, fewer missed-ETA complaints could reduce customer service costs. However, if premium customers perceive degraded service, churn risk increases — and premium customers disproportionately drive revenue. B2B contract violations could trigger penalty clauses.","operational":"Operations gains flexibility — dispatchers are no longer held to hour-level commitments, reducing route optimization pressure. However, this removes a valuable feedback signal: exact ETAs expose routing and capacity problems that date-only windows hide. Long-term, this could degrade operational discipline. KPI recalibration is required across reporting, dashboards, and SLA monitoring systems.","user":"Recipients lose planning precision. A timestamp lets a customer arrange to be home at 14:30; a date means staying home all day or risking a missed delivery. This is a significant UX regression for customers who valued the precision. Customer trust may erode if the change is perceived as the company hedging its commitments. Sender experience is also affected — senders using ETA to coordinate with recipients lose a key communication tool.","risk":"High risk of B2B contract breach if time-of-day SLAs exist. Risk of competitive disadvantage — industry trend is toward MORE precision (real-time tracking, 1-hour windows), not less. Risk of masking operational problems behind inflated KPIs. Risk of negative press if perceived as a service downgrade. Regulatory risk if operating in jurisdictions with consumer delivery time disclosure requirements.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End consumers (recipients)","impact":"Loss of delivery time precision reduces ability to plan around deliveries. Higher likelihood of missed deliveries due to inability to be present. Perceived service degradation."},{"group":"B2B / Enterprise customers","impact":"Potential SLA contract violations. Degraded reporting granularity. May need to renegotiate contracts or seek alternative carriers."},{"group":"Customer support teams","impact":"Short-term reduction in 'late delivery' complaints (wider window). But increase in 'when exactly will it arrive?' inquiries, potentially offsetting savings."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatch teams","impact":"Reduced pressure to meet exact times. Risk of declining delivery precision discipline over time."},{"group":"Product / Analytics teams","impact":"On-Time Delivery KPI becomes incomparable with historical data. Requires KPI recalibration and re-baselining."},{"group":"Sales / Account Management","impact":"Harder to sell premium tiers if ETA precision is visibly reduced. Competitive positioning weakened."},{"group":"Engineering / API consumers","impact":"Breaking change for downstream systems expecting datetime format. Requires API versioning strategy."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The proposal lacks critical information that was never provided: the motivation for the change (cost reduction? inaccurate ETAs? simplification?), existing SLA contracts and their precision requirements, which service tiers exist and their ETA commitments, whether this applies to all customers or specific segments, and whether the internal ETA calculation is also changing or only the display. Without knowing WHY this change is proposed, it is impossible to evaluate whether the trade-offs are justified.","high_confidence_areas":["ETA precision directly impacts customer experience and trust","Premium/express tiers are incompatible with date-only ETAs","On-Time Delivery KPI must be recalibrated if display precision changes","B2B API contracts must not silently change format"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether B2B contracts with time-of-day SLAs actually exist in this system","What service tiers are offered and their current ETA precision commitments","Whether the internal ETA model is also losing precision or only the display layer","The regulatory landscape for delivery time commitments in operating jurisdictions","Current On-Time Delivery Rate and whether timestamp precision is causing customer complaints"],"assumptions":["The system has multiple service tiers including at least standard and express/premium","Some B2B customers may have contractual SLA requirements around delivery time reporting","The change is display-only and the underlying ETA calculation still produces timestamps","The change would apply globally unless explicitly scoped to specific tiers or segments"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"The motivation for this change was never stated. Is it because exact ETAs are frequently inaccurate and causing complaints? Is it to reduce operational pressure? Cost reduction? Without the WHY, it is impossible to evaluate whether the trade-off is justified or to recommend alternatives (e.g., showing time windows like '2-4 PM' instead of exact timestamps).","affected_stakeholders":["Product team","Engineering team","All customer segments"],"options":["Proceed with analysis based on assumptions","Request the business case and motivation from the proposing team"],"recommendation":"Request the business case. The motivation fundamentally changes the analysis — if ETAs are wildly inaccurate, reducing displayed precision may be the right interim fix. If ETAs are accurate, this is a gratuitous service degradation."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information was provided about existing service tiers, their SLA definitions, or whether any tiers contractually guarantee time-of-day delivery precision. This is critical because the change may be safe for standard tiers but a contract violation for premium tiers.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B customers","Sales team","Legal team"],"options":["Assume all tiers are affected equally","Request service tier definitions and SLA contracts"],"recommendation":"Request the service tier catalog and associated SLA definitions before proceeding. A blanket change across all tiers carries significantly different risk than a scoped change to standard-only."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information about whether this change affects only the customer-facing display or also internal systems, APIs, and reporting. If the API response format changes from datetime to date-only, this is a breaking change for all downstream consumers.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering teams","B2B API consumers","Analytics teams"],"options":["Assume display-only change","Assume full-stack change including API"],"recommendation":"Clarify scope: display-only vs. API-level change. These have vastly different blast radii."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Blanket application across all service tiers including premium/express","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: ETA precision must be consistent with the service tier purchased. Date-only display is incompatible with premium service promises."},{"violation":"No migration strategy for in-flight deliveries","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A delivery promise, once communicated, is a commitment. Retroactively reducing precision on in-flight deliveries violates this invariant."}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes changing delivery ETA presentation from exact timestamps to date-only windows. This is a customer-facing change that affects how recipients and senders perceive delivery commitments. In logistics, ETA precision directly correlates with customer satisfaction, operational accountability, and contractual SLA enforcement. Reducing precision from timestamp to date-level is a significant domain change that affects the entire deliver

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this business change proposal about delivery ETA display format changes.

This is a domain analysis task - no code to read. Let me think through this carefully.

The proposal: Change from exact timestamps ("Delivery by 14:30 on Friday") to date-only windows ("Delivery by Friday").

This is an ambiguity case - the proposal is vague about WHY this change is being made, and "Delivery by Friday" could mean different things (by end of business Friday? by midnight Friday? anytime Friday?).

Let me classify: The key issue is that critical context is missing - we don't know WHY this change is proposed (is it because ETAs are inaccurate? to reduce customer complaints? operational flexibility?), what SLAs exist, what customer segments are affected, etc. 

The problem is that nobody's actually provided the reasoning behind this shift. I need to flag that we're missing essential information - the motivation for the change, current SLA commitments, which customer segments this affects, and what contractual obligations we're bound by. Without those details, I can't properly assess the impact.
